title.en: FiCR: Fire Compliance and Risk Analysis Ontology

description.en: FiCR, Fire Compliance and Risk Analysis, is a domain specific semantic model designed to represent fire safety and fire risk relevant physical objects, building elements, equipment, functional roles, and their relationships in existing buildings. It supports a structured and interoperable representation of both passive and active fire protection measures, as well as risk related attributes and performance outcomes, enabling compliance assessment, fire risk analysis, and inspection activities throughout the building operation and maintenance phase. FiCR further provides a semantic foundation for rule based reasoning and fire oriented digital twins that integrate regulatory compliance and risk informed decision making.

comments.en: FiCR, Fire Compliance and Risk Analysis Ontology

title.value: FiCR: Fire Compliance and Risk Analysis Ontology

comment.en: Includes two or more dwellings in the same building or on adjacent sites, designed and constructed as residential accommodation for vulnerable or elderly people who receive, or will receive, a support service.
A fire hazard severity classification for spaces with high fire load and combustibility.
A part of the physical world or a virtual world whose 3D spatial extent is bounded actually or theoretically, and provides for certain functions within the zone it is contained in.
Anything that exists in reality and can be located
Building planning of utilization.
Lighting for use when the power supply to the normal lighting fails.
A stair flight (or simply "flight") refers to an uninterrupted, continuous sequence of stairs (steps/risers) that connect two levels, floors, or intermediate landings.
An external wall door is a door that is part of a building's exterior wall, providing an entrance and exit to the building.
A building system refers to the interconnected, functional components of a building, including its mechanical, and electrical infrastructure.
A slab is the thick, structural concrete element (like reinforced concrete) forming a building's horizontal plane
Does not include a flat or a building containing a flat.

A part of the physical world or a virtual world that is inherently both located in this world and having a 3D spatial extent. A bot:Storey is contained in one or more buildings, and is intended to contain one or more spaces that are horizontally connected. Storeys of a building are connected by means of vertical connections such as elevators and stairs.
Includes any of the following.
• Any gallery in an assembly building (purpose group 5).
• Any gallery in any other type of building if its area is more than half that of the space into which it projects.
• A roof, unless it is accessible only for maintenance and repair.
A "hall" in a building can refer to a large, central room or a long, narrow passageway.
A compliance result indicating that the assessed element or system does not meet the applicable regulatory requirement.

Firefighting in buildings is the strategic and tactical process used by the fire and rescue service to control and extinguish fires, protect lives and property, and manage associated hazards within a built structure.

Escape routes that are sufficiently separated by direction and space or by fire resisting construction to ensure that one is still available if the other is affected by fire.
A second stair, balcony or flat roof which enables a person to reach a place free from danger from fire is considered an alternative escape route for the purposes of a dwellinghouse.
Assumption that internal cavities are adequately sealed by cavity barriers and do not provide a path for fire spread.
A fabric element category for components that close openings in the building envelope or internal partitions, including doors, windows, and dampers.
In buildings, an "engine room" typically refers to the central location for the mechanical and electrical systems that keep the building running, such as HVAC, boilers, generators, and pumps.
A window is an opening in a building wall for light and air, featuring casements or sashes with glass panes, enhancing natural lighting, ventilation, and aesthetics.
An assessment that determines whether requirements derived from a regulatory source are satisfied, producing a compliance result and justification.
A fire resisting floor to separate one fire compartment from another.
Car parks designed to admit and accommodate only cars, motorcycles and passenger or light goods vehicles that weigh a maximum of 2500kg gross.

Either of the following:
• place (other than described under 7(b)) for the storage or deposit of goods or materials
• any building not within purpose groups 1 to 6.
A space usage for rooms primarily housing building services equipment and infrastructure.
A flat is a separate and self-contained premises constructed or adapted for use for residential purposes and forming part of a building from some other part of which it is divided horizontally.
Sub-structure component, typically concrete, brick, or masonry, that transfers loads from the building's walls down to the ground or a concrete footing

A final exit, or a doorway that gives direct access into a protected stairway, firefighting lobby or external escape route.
NOTE: If an institutional building is planned to enable progressive horizontal evacuation, a door in a compartment wall is considered a storey exit for the purposes of requirement B1.

Offices or premises used for any of the following and their control:
• administration
• clerical work (including writing, bookkeeping, sorting papers, filing, typing, duplicating, machine calculating, drawing and the editorial preparation of matter for publication, police and fire and rescue service work)
• handling money (including banking and building society work)
• communications (including postal, telegraph and radio communications)
• radio, television, film, audio or video recording
• performance (premises not open to the public).
A lift that may be used to evacuate people in a fire.
A utilization purpose for premises used for entertainment or recreational activities.
A balcony is a platform projecting from the wall of a building, enclosed by a balustrade or railing, and accessed from an upper-level room.

A corridor is a long, narrow passageway in a building that connects different rooms or areas.
A design of a building containing flats, in which each flat is approached via a common horizontal internal access or circulation space, which may include a common entrance hall.
A trigger event generated from routine schedules or planned inspection cycles derived from FiCR maintenance and regulatory schedules, such as monthly fire exit checks.
A place of education for children between 2 and 19 years old. The term includes nursery schools, primary schools and secondary schools as defined in the Education Act 1996.
Describes a system or element that actively detects or suppresses fire, such as sprinkler systems, fire alarm circuits, and emergency lighting systems.

A hall or space inside a dwelling that connects
the entrance door to the rooms.
A task that estimates insurance-relevant risk metrics such as EML and PML by combining asset exposure, compliance outcomes, and supporting evidence.
A sensor in a building is a device that detects environmental changes and sends data to a control system to manage building functions like lighting, HVAC, and safety.
"Fire safety space" in a building refers to the areas designed to prevent the spread of fire and smoke and guide occupants to safety.
A device attached to the external surface of an external wall for reducing heat gain within a building by shading or deflecting sunlight.

Classify buildings or compartments according to their intended use, construction type, or structural risk to ensure safety, regulatory compliance, and facilitate communication between professionals such as planners, insurers, and contractors.
The part of the emergency lighting that is provided to ensure that the escape route is illuminated at all material times.
A flat is a separate and self-contained premises constructed or adapted for use for residential purposes and forming part of a building from some other part of which it is divided horizontally.
Installation and specification status of fire alarm systems.
Dwellinghouse that does not contain a habitable storey with a floor level a minimum of 4.5m above ground level.
Movable items that make a space habitable and decorative.
Dwellinghouse that contains a habitable storey with a floor level a minimum of 4.5m above ground level up to a maximum of 18m.

A switchgear room in a building is a dedicated space that houses electrical equipment used to control, protect, and isolate the building's electrical system, such as circuit breakers, switches, and fuses.

A space enclosed by elements of a building (including a suspended ceiling) or contained within an element, but that is not a room, cupboard, circulation space, protected shaft, or space within a flue, chute, duct, pipe or conduit.
Fuel storage in buildings refers to the secure and safe storage of various fuels, such as oil, petrol, or wood, for heating, cooking, or other purposes.
Hotel, boarding house, residential college, hall of residence, hostel or any other residential purpose not described above.

Hospital, home, school or other similar establishment, where people sleep on the premises. The building may be either of the following:
• Living accommodation for, or accommodation for the treatment, care or maintenance of, either:
    - disabled people with a range of impairments including physical, sensory and
cognitive impairments, or mental health conditions
    - people under the age of 5 years.
• A place of lawful detention.

A configuration node representing a performance requirement derived from regulatory sources (e.g. ADB, BDM02). Instances such as req_pg1b_wall carry the required REI value for a given purpose group and element type, and serve as the benchmark in SPARQL compliance queries. Actual performance is expressed directly on physical elements via hasREI and similar data properties.
A mechanical or intumescent device within a duct or ventilation opening that operates automatically and is designed to resist the spread of fire.
Combination of components for giving an audible and/or other perceptible warning of fire.
Access role refers to the room that the only escape route from an inner room passes through.
A fire hazard severity classification for spaces with low fire load and combustibility.
A room for food preparation.
Residential other than Dwellings.

A dwelling is a unit where one or more people live (whether or not as a sole or main residence) in either of the following situations.
• A single person or people living together as a family.
• A maximum of six people living together as a single household, including where care is provided for residents.
The operational state cannot be determined.
Storage and Car Parking

Shops or premises used for either of the following.
• A retail trade or business (including selling food or drink to the public for immediate consumption, retail by auction, self-selection and over-the-counter wholesale trading, the business of lending books or periodicals for gain, the business of a barber or hairdresser, and the rental of storage space to the public).
• Premises to which the public are invited either:
    – to deliver or collect goods in connection with their hire, repair or other treatment
    – (except in the case of repair of motor vehicles) where the public themselves may carry out such repairs or other treatments.

Offices or premises used for any of the following and their control:
• administration
• clerical work (including writing, bookkeeping, sorting papers, filing, typing, duplicating, machine calculating, drawing and the editorial preparation of matter for publication, police and fire and rescue service work)
• handling money (including banking and building society work)
• communications (including postal, telegraph and radio communications)
• radio, television, film, audio or video recording
• performance (premises not open to the public).

Factories and other premises used for any of the following:
• manufacturing, altering, repairing, cleaning, washing, breaking up, adapting or processing any article
• generating power
• slaughtering livestock.

Place of assembly, entertainment or recreation, including any of the following:
• bingo halls, broadcasting, recording and film studios open to the public, casinos, dance halls
• entertainment, conference, exhibition and leisure centres
• funfairs and amusement arcades
• museums and art galleries, non-residential clubs, theatres, cinemas, concert halls
• educational establishments, dancing schools, gymnasia, swimming pool buildings, riding schools, skating rinks, sports pavilions, sports stadia
• law courts
• churches and other buildings of worship, crematoria
• libraries open to the public, non-residential day centres, clinics, health centres and surgeries
• passenger stations and termini for air, rail, road or sea travel
• public toilets
• zoos and menageries.

A device that closes a door, when open at any angle, against a door frame.
NOTE: If the door is in a cavity barrier, rising butt hinges (which are different from the self-closing device mentioned above) are acceptable.
A trigger event generated when abnormal conditions are detected by monitoring or analytics, causing tasks to be created for verification, inspection, or risk follow-up.
A horizontal structural slab forming the topmost floor of a building, directly supporting the roof covering and transferring loads to walls or columns.
A storey at or above ground level, defined by isAboveGround = true.

The end of an escape route from a building that gives direct access to a street, passageway, walkway or open space, and is sited to ensure that people rapidly disperse away from the building so that they are no longer in danger from fire and/or smoke.
Windows are not acceptable as final exits.

An external file or record that supports assessment or documentation, such as product data sheets, installation manuals, certificates, drawings, or inspection reports. It is linked for human access and traceability and does not directly participate in logical inference unless explicitly modelled.
RiskUnit is an exposure value aggregation unit in insurance assessment.

Unprotected area (in relation to a side or external wall of a building) All of the following are classed as unprotected areas.
• Any part of the external wall that has less than the relevant fire resistance set out in Section 11.
• Any part of the external wall constructed of material more than 1mm thick if that material does not have a class B-s3, d2 rating or better, which is attached or applied, whether for cladding or any other purpose.
• Windows, doors or other openings. This does not include windows that are designed and glazed to give the necessary level of fire resistance and that are not openable.
NOTE: Recessed car parking areas as shown in
Diagram A1 should not be regarded as unprotected
areas.
a stairway (or staircase) is a series of steps connecting different levels in a building, serving as a pedestrian route.

A stairway space, also called a stairwell, is the vertical compartment in a building that contains a flight of stairs and the associated landings, hallways, or other areas needed to move between floors.
The boundary of the land that belongs to a building, or, where the land abuts a road, railway, canal or river, the centre line of that road, railway, canal or river.
An assessment outcome produced by executing tasks, supported by evidence, and traceable to triggering events.
Abstract analytical concepts for spatial relationships and paths, not physical entities.
Installation status indicating whether an automatic sprinkler system is installed and to what specification.
A lobby is the large, open space at the main entrance of a building that serves as an entry hall, gathering area, and passageway.
A column is a vertical structural element in a building that supports loads from above and transfers them down to the foundation.
A railing refers to a protective barrier or fence, usually made of metal (such as wrought or cast iron, steel, or aluminium), timber, or plastic, designed to provide safety and support.
A condition state indicating that the physical performance of a boundary element is intact and functioning as designed.
The boundary or notional boundary that one side of the building faces and/or coincides with, and that is parallel or at an angle of a maximum of 80 degrees to that side of the building.
A workflow task in FiCR. Tasks organise how evidence is collected, how compliance is checked against regulations, and how risk is estimated for insurance-related analysis.
A boiler room is a dedicated space in a building that houses essential mechanical equipment for services like heating, hot water, and electricity
A corridor that is adequately protected from fire in adjoining areas by fire resisting construction.
An HVAC system is a building's climate control system for heating, ventilation, and air conditioning.
Fixed firefighting systems. Automatic sprinkler systems. Design, installation and maintenance.

Fire Protection of Buildings
RISCAuthority BDM02 - Fire Protection of Buildings - 2022 v2 A4

The Building Risk Evaluation Tool (BRET), is intended to provide an easy to follow insight into how the use, design, and construction material choices can influence the resilience of a building to the challenges presented by fire in terms of property protection, business resilience, and insurability. It may provide benefit to designers during the initial development stages looking to ensure the end result will meet the resilience expectations of the occupier and be insurable, and to insurers triaging new prospects.

Approved Document B Volume 1&2
Building regulation in England covering fire safety matters within and around buildings.
Save relevant knowledge documents from the UK.
A continuous pathway that allows electricity to flow from the main supply (consumer unit) to various outlets, lights, or appliances, and back again.
A skylight, glazed barrel vault or other element to admit daylight through a roof.
Describe the uses of the space.
Physical performance status assessment of boundary condition state.

Evidence collected from the building or its digital sensors during inspection or monitoring, such as RGB images, depth, LiDAR/point clouds, and sensor readings. This class represents the content used for assessment, not the provenance metadata standard itself.
a room for washing and personal hygiene, typically containing a toilet, sink, and either a bathtub or shower.

A continuous space that passes through one or more structural floors within a building, not necessarily vertically.
Enclosed lift wells, enclosed escalator wells, building services ducts and stairs are not classified as atria.
A utilization purpose for premises providing lodging and meals for paying guests.

comment.undefined: The large area where people meet or wait just inside the main doors of a building.

comment.en: Classification of a building or compartment by its intended use according to ADB Table 0.1.
A task that evaluates one or more requirements against available evidence and produces a compliance result.
A fire safety system in buildings is a collection of equipment and practices designed to detect, alert occupants of, and control or extinguish fires, thereby protecting lives and property.
Inner room refers to Room from which escape is possible only by passing through another room (the access room).

A transformer room is a dedicated space within a building that houses transformers and associated electrical equipment to step down high-voltage electricity from the grid to a usable low voltage for the building.
A fire safety role specifically assigned to physical building elements via the hasElementUsage property, forming the basis of the semantic bridging pattern.
A fire hazard severity classification for spaces with moderate fire load and combustibility.
The system is out of service, degraded, or bypassed.

The different types of assumptions relied upon include: floor partitions prevent vertical spread, internal cavities do not spread fire, external walls do not cause external spread, and building structural stability.
A space usage for areas primarily serving pedestrian movement, such as corridors, halls, and lobbies.
Physical building elements.
An auditorium is a large room or building designed for public gatherings, such as performances, lectures, or meetings.
A structural element is a component of a building that is essential for its stability and for transferring loads to the ground, such as foundations, beams, columns, and load-bearing walls.
The system is functioning as designed.
A horizontal structural slab forming an intermediate or ground-level floor, transferring occupancy and dead loads to the supporting structure.
A doorset is a complete, integrated door system consisting of the door leaf, frame, and all necessary hardware like hinges, seals, and closers, sold as a single unit from one source.

Includes any of the following.
• A balcony attached to an external wall.
• A device for reducing heat gain within a building by deflecting sunlight which is attached to an external wall.
Structural means that provide one or more safe routes for people to go, during a fire, from any point in the building to a place of safety.
A corridor or lobby that is adequately protected from fire in adjoining areas by fire resisting construction.

An independent unit of the built environment with a characteristic spatial structure, intended to serve at least one function or user activity [ISO 12006-2:2013]. A bot:Building is a part of the physical world or a virtual world that is inherently both located in this world and having a 3D spatial extent, is contained in a building site, and can contain one or more storeys that are vertically connected.
Any permanent or temporary building but not any other kind of structure or erection. A reference to a building includes a reference to part of a building.
Cladding is an external layer applied to a building's structure to provide weather protection, thermal insulation, and improved aesthetics.
A seal provided to close an imperfection of fit or design tolerance between elements or components, to restrict the spread of fire and smoke.
Installation and specification status of fire dampers within ductwork.
A utilization purpose for premises used for public assembly, entertainment, or recreation.
A compliance result indicating that the compliance status could not be determined from available evidence.

A storey with a floor that, at some point, is more than 1200mm below the highest level of ground beside the outside walls. (However, see Appendix B, paragraph B26c, for situations where the storey is considered to be a basement only because of a sloping site.)
A compliance result indicating that the assessed element or system meets the applicable regulatory requirement.
Finishes are the final surface treatments and decorative elements applied to a building's interior and exterior.
A lift shaft is a vertical passageway in a building where an elevator travels between floors.

A stair that leads to a final exit to a place of safety and that is adequately enclosed with fire resisting construction. Included in the definition is any exit passageway between the foot of the stair and the final exit.
An electrical circuit that is protected against fire.
A floor is the horizontal surface in a building that forms the base of a room, providing structural support for occupants, furniture, and the building's own weight.
A fire protection system in a building is a coordinated set of equipment and procedures designed to detect, control, and extinguish fires to safeguard lives and property.
A fire safety role specifically assigned to building zones via the hasZoneFireSafetyRole property, enabling zone-level semantic classification.

A part of the physical world or a virtual world that is inherently both located in this world and has a 3D spatial extent; Sub-classes of bot:Zone include bot:Site, bot:Building, bot:Storey, or bot:Space.  An instance of bot:Zone can contain other bot:Zone instances, making it possible to group or subdivide zones. An instance of bot:Zone can be adjacent to or intersecting other bot:Zone instances. Finally, a bot:Zone can instantiate three relations to bot:Element, which are either contained in (bot:containsElement), adjacent to it (bot:adjacentElement) or intersecting (bot:intersectingElement).
A utilization purpose for premises providing medical treatment, care, or maintenance of persons.

An enclosed space within a building that is not used solely as a circulation space. The term includes not only conventional rooms, but also cupboards that are not fittings and large spaces such as warehouses and auditoria. The term does not include cavities such as ducts, ceiling cavities and roof spaces.
A beam is a horizontal structural element that carries loads perpendicular to its length, transferring weight from a roof, floor, or ceiling to vertical supports like columns or walls.

A fire resisting wall to separate one fire compartment from another.
including a wall that is common to two or more buildings.
Includes pipe fittings and accessories. The definition of ‘pipe’ excludes a flue pipe and a pipe used for ventilating purposes, other than a ventilating pipe for an above-ground drainage system.
A floor or balcony that does not extend across the full extent of a building’s footprint and is open to the floor below.
A circulation area, consisting of a hall or space in a flat, that is enclosed with fire resisting construction other than an external wall of a building.

The outcome of a compliance assessment, representing a discrete compliance status such as compliant, non-compliant, or undetermined. It is produced by a ComplianceAssessment via hasComplianceResult and can be consumed by a FireRiskAssessmentTask via usesComplianceResult to inform risk calculations.
A condition state indicating that the physical condition of a boundary element cannot be determined from available evidence.

A room such as any of the following.
• Oil-filled transformer room.
• Switch gear room.
• Boiler room.
• Storage space for fuel or other highly flammable substance(s).
• Room that houses a fixed internal combustion engine.
A utilization purpose for institutional residential accommodation, typically for students or workers.
Whether a fire safety system or component is installed, and if so, to what specification.
A roof space is the area inside a building between the ceiling of the top floor and the underside of the roof.
Inspection task consumes observations produced by upstream tools.

The external wall of a building includes all of the following.
• Anything located within any space forming part of the wall.
• Any decoration or other finish applied to any external (but not internal) surface forming part of the wall.
• Any windows and doors in the wall.
• Any part of a roof pitched at an angle of more than 70 degrees to the horizontal if that part of the roof adjoins a space within the building to which persons have access, but not access only for the purpose of carrying out repairs or maintenance.
Assumption that the building structure maintains load-bearing stability for the required period during a fire event.

A trigger event created when a compliance request is received from an external authority, or when a prior task identifies missing evidence or unmet preconditions requiring follow-up inspection or assessment.
Circulation space refers to a space (including a protected stairway) mainly used as a means of access between a room and an exit from the building or compartment.

A referenced regulatory or standards document used as the normative basis for a check or requirement, such as Approved Document B, BDM02, or related standards. It provides the requirement context and should be versioned where possible.

Shops or premises used for either of the following.
• A retail trade or business (including selling food or drink to the public for immediate consumption, retail by auction, self-selection and over the-counter wholesale trading, the business of lending books or periodicals for gain, the business of a barber or hairdresser, and the rental of storage space to the public).
• Premises to which the public are invited either:
– to deliver or collect goods in connection with their hire, repair or other treatment
– (except in the case of repair of motor vehicles) where the public themselves may carry out such repairs or other treatments.

Part of a building that encloses a room, protected shaft or circulation space and is exposed overhead.
The soffit of a rooflight, but not the frame, is included as part of the surface of the ceiling. An upstand below a rooflight is considered as a wall.
Assumption that the external wall construction does not contribute to external fire spread between compartments or buildings.
A warehouse is a large building used for storing goods, such as raw materials, finished products, or equipment, until they are needed for manufacturing, distribution, or sale.
Furniture, equipment, inventory - not part of building structure.
Classification of building, space or component usage.

A stairway space that leads to a final exit to a place of safety and that is adequately enclosed with fire resisting construction. Included in the definition is any exit passageway between the foot of the stair and the final exit.
A utilization purpose for premises providing temporary or permanent residential living.
A lift with additional protection and with controls that enable it to be used by the fire and rescue service when fighting a fire.
One of two or more exits, each of which is separate from the other.

A construction within a cavity, other than a smoke curtain, to perform either of the
following functions.
• Close a cavity to stop smoke or flame entering.
• Restrict the movement of smoke or flame within a cavity.
In buildings, a "controller" is the "brain" of an automated system, receiving data from sensors and sending commands to devices like HVAC, lighting, and alarms to ensure comfort and efficiency.
An occurrence that triggers the creation of one or more tasks in the FiCR workflow, such as a routine schedule, an anomaly alert, or a compliance request.
A service shaft is a vertical passage in a building that houses pipes, cables, and ducts for essential building systems like water, electricity, and ventilation.

The building is regarded as a multi-storey building if both of the following apply.
• There is more than one gallery.
• The total aggregate area of all the galleries in one space is more than half the floor area of that space.
A shaft that enables people, air or objects to pass from one compartment to another, and which is enclosed with fire resisting construction.
Complete machinery and infrastructure for vertical transport.
An external wall window is a window that is part of a building's exterior wall, which acts as the outer boundary separating the interior from the outside environment.
A utilization purpose for premises used for commercial trading activities.

A building that consists of a ground storey only. Basements are not counted as storeys in a building (see Appendix D). A separated part that consists of a ground storey only, with a roof to which access is only provided for repair or maintenance, may be treated as a single storey building.

A door or shutter which, together with its frame and furniture as installed in a building, is intended (when closed) to resist the spread of fire and/or gaseous products of combustion and meets specified performance criteria to those ends.
An alarm in a building is a system designed to alert occupants to a potential danger, such as fire or intrusion, and consists of detectors, warning devices, and control equipment.
Indicates fire loss boundary
A building risk assessment is a systematic process of identifying, evaluating, and managing potential hazards and their associated risks within a building.
Fire hazard severity classification: light, moderate, or severe.
Assumption that floor and wall partitions maintain fire compartmentation and prevent vertical or horizontal fire spread.
A wall in buildings is a vertical structure that defines and separates spaces, provides shelter and security, and can be either load-bearing or non-load-bearing.
A protected passageway that connects a protected stairway to a final exit.
The route along which people can escape from any point in a building to a final exit.
A utilization purpose for a college providing on-site residential accommodation for students.

Describes an element or space that is passively protected by fire-resisting construction, such as a protected circuit with fire-rated cable, a stair enclosed by fire-resisting walls, or a protected corridor.

A hypothetical fire scenario anchored to a specific origin space (via hasOriginSpace). It models fire ignition, horizontal and vertical spread pathways, and resulting loss exposure. Each space in the building can serve as the origin of a distinct scenario, and a RiskAssessment evaluates one or more scenarios via hasScenario.

A single piece of evidence that supports an assessment. It may be an observation, a regulatory reference, or an external document. Evidence items are linkable, traceable, and can be attached to specific assets, spaces, or checks.
A duct to deliver combustion air to a gas appliance.
The land occupied by the building, up to the boundaries with land in other ownership.
ImpairmentState of the building component.

An electrical system in a building is a network of wiring, equipment, and protective devices that safely distributes power from its entry point to all the loads, such as lights, outlets, and appliances, within the structure.
An area from which escape is possible in one direction only.

A device that normally holds a door open, but closes it automatically if any one of the following occurs.
• Smoke is detected by an automatic device of a suitable nature and quality in a suitable location.
• A hand-operated switch, fitted in a suitable position, is operated.
• The electricity supply to the device, apparatus or switch fails.
• The fire alarm system, if any, is operated.
A utilization purpose for premises providing temporary guest accommodation with services.

A configuration node recording the minimum performance value mandated by regulation for a specific element type and purpose group. Instances such as req_pg1b_wall carry the required REI for walls in PG1b, linked to their scope via appliesToElementType and appliesToPurposeGroup.
A Vertical Transport System (VT) in buildings refers to the equipment and planning used to move people and goods vertically between different floors.

same as Fire Resistance
The ability of a component or a building to satisfy, for a stated period of time, some or all of the appropriate criteria given in the relevant standard.
Assumption that the external wall construction does not contribute to external fire spread between compartments or buildings.
A condition state indicating that the physical performance of a boundary element is degraded or unreliable.
A utilization purpose for premises providing basic shared accommodation.

A generic concept to qualify the relationship of two or more things in the world, where at least one is a building element or zone. Examples:
    - Qualification of heat transmission between zones through one or more building elements. This includes one-dimensional (surface) heat losses from one zone to another through a single building element, a two dimensional (line) loss from one zone to another through the connection in which the two elements meet or a three dimensional (point) loss from one zone to another through the connection where three elements (typically two walls and a slab) meet. 
    - Connection of an electric device to the electric system of a building. 
    - A door between one room and another.
A utilization purpose for premises used for lawful detention of persons.
A dome light, lantern light, ridge light.
A utilization purpose for premises designed for vehicle parking and storage.
Part of a solar shading device which is set in motion by the operating system and fulfils the purpose of a blind, awning or shutter.
A compartment wall, compartment floor, cavity barrier and construction that encloses a protected escape route and/or a place of special fire hazard.

A smaller, enclosed vertical space extending through multiple floors used for specific building services, such as elevator hoistways, enclosed stairwells, or ducts for plumbing, electrical, or air-conditioning equipment.
A room used, or intended to be used, for people to live in (including, for the purposes of Approved Document B Volumes 1 and 2, a kitchen, but not a bathroom).
A utilization purpose for premises used for storing goods or materials.

A portable or fixed manually-operated device containing an extinguishing agent that can be expelled for the purpose of suppressing or extinguishing a fire. Fire extinguishers are classified by their agent type (water, foam, CO2, dry powder, wet chemical) and by the fire classes they are rated to address.
A fire safety space bounded by compartment walls and compartment floors, designed to prevent the spread of fire and smoke beyond the compartment of origin.
A fire damper which, in addition to the performance of the fire damper, resists the spread of smoke.
Refers to any intentional void in walls, floors, or ceilings for access, light, air, or services, such as doors, windows, ducts, or stairwells.

A bundle of evidence items used to justify a fire compliance evaluation or risk assessment. It groups observed site evidence, regulatory sources, and supporting external documents for traceability and explanation.
Independently identifiable. Maintainable/inspectable. Usually has a model/serial number. Replaceable physical device or apparatus.
Building structural components. Aligning the BuildingElement with the BOT.
A duct space is a designated area or channel within a building that houses the systems for conveying air, cables, and other utilities.
A roof is the top covering of a building that provides protection from the elements like rain, sun, snow, and wind.
The date by which the equipment must be serviced or replaced to remain compliant.
Total gross floor area of all storeys in a building, in square metres.
Fire resistance period expressed as R (load-bearing), E (integrity), I (insulation), in minutes.
Links a task to evidence items it consumes as inputs.
A human-readable title used to display the document in user interfaces.
Inverse of hasElementUsage.
General property linking an entity to a fire safety role, parent of hasElementUsage and hasZoneFireSafetyRole.
Links a building system or component to its current operational impairment state.
Specifies the class of physical element this requirement applies to, e.g. ficr:Wall or ficr:Floor.
The volume of a space, in cubic metres.
A task is created because an event (e.g., risk detected) occurred.
Expressing the compositional relationship of components
The spatial coverage mode of the detector, e.g. PointDetector, BeamDetector, AspiratingDetector.
Links an assessment activity or assessment result to the evidence bundle that supports it.
Refers to the state of being obscured.
The floor-to-floor height of a storey, in metres.
Measured physical separation distance in metres between two risk units.
A standard monetary unit value per square metre used in insurance risk calculations.
Relationship between two zones that share a common interface, but do not intersect.

Links an assessment to the zone(s) or space(s) it covers. For risk assessments, this is the space whose fire scenario is being evaluated. For compliance assessments, this is the space containing the assessed elements.
Inverse of hasFireSafetyRole.

Links a Zone to an Element that is either contained in, adjacent to or intersecting with the Zone. The intended use of this relationship is not to be stated explicitly, but to be inferred from its sub-properties. It will, for example, allow one to query for all the doors of a building given that they have an adjacency to spaces of the building.
The width dimension of a building element, in metres.

The shortest distance from any point within the floor area to the nearest storey exit, measured within the external enclosures of the building, and ignoring walls, partitions and fittings other than the enclosing walls and partitions to protected stairways.
Links an impairment state to the building system whose operation is degraded.

Links a bot:Site to an instance that encodes the latitude and longitude of the Zero Point of the building site. This could be an instance of a wgs84:Point. The definition of GIS and geometry is not within the scope of BOT and an appropriate ontology needs to be selected here by the user. The use of this property is potentially ambiguous and it might be removed or revised in future editions of the ontology.
Inverse of hasClassification.
Assigns a building planning utilization purpose (e.g. Hotel, Office) to a zone or building.
Links a boundary assumption or impairment state to a physical condition state assessment.
Inverse of hasUtilizationPurpose.
Links any bot:Zone or bot:Element to a 3D Model encoded as a literal.
The vertical height of a storey floor level above a reference datum, in metres.
Links a compliance checking assessment to the compliance result it produces.
Relates a building to its functional purpose group classification according to ADB Table 0.1
Links a task to assessment outcomes produced by executing it.
This refers to the total asset value of the space.
Relation to storeys contained in a zone. The typical domains of bot:hasStorey are instances of bot:Building.
Links a boundary assumption to the category of fire loss assumption relied upon.

Links any bot:Zone or bot:Element to a IRI that identifies its 3D Model. This 3D Model can then be described using some dedicated RDF vocabulary. Else, the 3D Model IRI could be dereferenceable, and when looking up the IRI one could retrieve a representation of the 3D Model with some existing data format for 3D models.
Top-level property linking an entity to a usage type classification. Sub-properties specialise domain and range.
The actual measured fire resistance period (R=load-bearing, E=integrity, I=insulation) in minutes.
Inverse of hasSpaceUsage.
The scheduled start date and time of a task.
The fire class rating per BS EN 2, e.g. A, B, AB, ABC, AF.
Determine whether the storey is a basement or above ground.
indicates that the two individuals have a passageway.
describe the location and/or surroundings of a place.
Links an individual equipment item to the building system it belongs to. Inverse direction of hasComponent at the instance level.

Links an assessment to the physical element(s) it evaluates. For compliance assessments, this is the wall, door, or floor being checked. For risk assessments, this covers elements involved in fire spread modelling.

Relationship to the subzones of a major zone. A space zone could for instance be contained in a storey zone which is further contained in a building zone. bot:containsZone is a transitive property. This implies that in the previous example a bot:containsZone relationship holds between the space zone and the building zone.
Boolean indicating whether the element or space is fully enclosed by construction.
Indicates potential fire spread exposure between risk units.
The minimum REI value mandated by regulation for a given element type and purpose group, in minutes.
The length dimension of a building element, in metres.
Number of storeys excluding basements
The floor-to-ceiling height of the topmost storey, in metres.
The floor area of a space in square metres.
Boolean indicating whether the element carries structural loads.
Inverse of hasUsageType.
A resolvable URI or file path pointing to the document location for human access.
Links a compliance assessment to the regulatory source used to derive the requirement(s).
Links a zone to its fire hazard severity classification (light, moderate, or severe).
Inverse of hasFireRiskUnitRole.
Links a boundary assumption or impairment state to the risk unit it influences.
Links a boundary assumption or impairment state to the evidence items that justify it.
Inverse of hasPurposeGroup.
Relationship between two zones whose 3D extent intersect. For example, a stairwell intersects different storeys.
Must be subject to inspection assignments (e.g., fire safety inspections).
Inverse of hasZoneFireSafetyRole.
Relation to buildings contained in a zone. The typical domains of bot:hasBuilding are instances of bot:Site.
Links a fire risk scenario to the zone where the fire is modelled to originate.
General property relating an entity to its classification.
Height of a building is measured as shown in Appendix D, Diagram D4.
The scheduled end date and time of a task.
Height of the floor of the top storey above ground level is measured as shown in Appendix D, Diagram D6.
The value of a physical object, such as the value of furniture.
Boolean indicating whether the element forms part of the external building envelope.
Relation between a Zone and a building Element that intersects it.

(unless otherwise specified, e.g. as in the case of flats) The distance that a person would travel from any point within the floor area to the nearest storey exit, determined by the layout of walls, partitions and fittings.
The extinguishing agent type, e.g. DryPowder, CO2, WaterMist, WetChemical, Foam.
A unique identifier string for the entity.
Indicates that the subject storey is directly below the object storey.
Links a risk unit to a separation distance record describing its exposure to an adjacent risk unit.
Links a risk unit to the spatial zone whose fire exposure it aggregates.
Links a task to evidence items it generates.
Indicates that the subject storey is directly above the object storey.
Assigns a fire safety role to a building zone, enabling zone-level equivalentClass definitions for protected spaces.
The detection principle of the alarm device, e.g. OpticalSmoke, Ionisation, Heat, MultiSensor, CarbonMonoxide.
Links a boundary assumption to the physical element whose fire performance it assesses.
Assigns a fire safety role to a physical element, indicating the element's functional contribution to fire protection (e.g. fire separating, fire resisting, passive fire protection).
Indicates whether this system is installed and to what specification.

Links a risk assessment to the fire risk scenario(s) it evaluates. Each scenario represents a hypothetical fire origin and its modelled spread, corresponding to the per-room EML calculations in the FiCR risk model.
Minimum separation distance in metres required to prevent fire spread, computed per NFPA 80A. Stored here as an externally-supplied value; FiCR does not compute this internally.
Assigns a fire risk unit role to an entity.
Relationship between an interface and another thing (building zone, element or owl:Thing)
Boolean indicating whether the element shows visible damage affecting its fire performance.
Links a trigger event to the task(s) created because of it.
Specifies the building purpose group for which this requirement is applicable.
The thickness dimension of a building element, in metres.
Links a risk assessment to compliance results used as inputs to risk estimation.
Assigns a functional space usage classification (e.g. Kitchen, Corridor) to a zone or space.
